So how could   this happen? I don=E2=80=99t know, but let me speculate a li=
ttle bit.
Let=E2=80=99s start with Coinbase. Coinbase Global Inc. runs a cryptocurren=
cy exchange. When FTX.com, one of the largest crypto exchanges, was instant=
aneously vaporized yesterday,  Coinbase put out a statement, the gist of wh=
ich was =E2=80=9Cdon=E2=80=99t worry,=C2=A0we are not going to be instantan=
eously vaporized.=E2=80=9D The part that I want to focus on is this paragra=
ph:
There can=E2=80=99t be a =E2=80=9Crun on the bank=E2=80=9D at Coinbase. As =
you can review in our publicly filed, audited financial statements, we hold=
 customer assets 1:1. Any institutional lending activity at Coinbase is at =
the discretion of the customer and backed by collateral. We have no gating =
for client loan recalls or withdrawals.
The way it works is roughly that you open an account and send dollars to Co=
inbase, and then you tell Coinbase =E2=80=9CI=E2=80=99d like to buy some Bi=
tcoin with those dollars,=E2=80=9D and Coinbase buys Bitcoin and holds on t=
o it for you=C2=A0and  charges you a fee for that transaction. You can chec=
k your account balance, and Coinbase says =E2=80=9Cyou have 0.5 Bitcoin=E2=
=80=9D or whatever. That 0.5=C2=A0Bitcoin is, in the general case,=C2=A0hel=
d by Coinbase; it has possession of the Bitcoin.
  [1]
=C2=A0But it is held in a custody account=C2=A0for you.  Coinbase says:

Your funds are your funds, and your crypto is your crypto: Coinbase maintai=
ns internal systems, like a bank or a broker. Our fully audited ledger iden=
tifies your account, your fiat and crypto holdings, and tracks your account=
 activity in real time. There=E2=80=99s never a situation where customer fu=
nds could be confused with corporate assets.
We will never repurpose your funds: We do not lend or take any action with =
your assets, unless you specifically instruct us to. Many banks and financi=
al institutions use customer funds for commercial purposes including lendin=
g and trading, meaning that they often hold only a fraction of their custom=
er assets at any given time. Coinbase always holds customer assets 1:1. Thi=
s means that funds are available to our customers 24 hours a day, 7 days a =
week, 365 days of the year.

The analogy is: Imagine a weird sort of bank. You come to the bank with $10=
0 in paper bills, and you deposit it in the bank, and the bank takes your p=
aper bills and sticks them in an envelope with your name on it. Then it sti=
cks the envelope in a vault, and if at any point you ask for your money bac=
k, it opens the vault and hands you your envelope. This sounds like a bad b=
usiness model: The bank needs to pay for real estate and tellers and vaults=
, and it is not doing anything with your money. But the other weird thing a=
bout this bank is that, every day, you come in and say =E2=80=9Chey I=E2=80=
=99d like to exchange my dollars for euros=E2=80=9D or =E2=80=9Cmy euros fo=
r pounds=E2=80=9D or whatever, and each time you do that the bank charges y=
ou a dollar. So you have $100, which you exchange for =E2=82=AC99, which yo=
u exchange for =C2=A398, which you exchange for $97, etc.,
  [2]
=C2=A0paying the bank $1 each time. If all of the bank=E2=80=99s customers =
do this every day, then the bank makes plenty of money to pay for real esta=
te and tellers and vaults and executive bonuses, without doing anything els=
e with your money. It just takes the $100 out of your envelope and replaces=
 it with =E2=82=AC99, etc., always keeping exactly the right amount of mone=
y (in whatever currency you like that day) in exactly your envelope.
  [3]

And then if one day every single customer walked into the bank at the same =
time and said =E2=80=9Cwe would like our money back,=E2=80=9D the bank woul=
d just hand them all their envelopes. Don=E2=80=99t get me wrong, this woul=
d be a catastrophe for the bank: If everyone took their envelopes back, the=
n presumably they would stop changing money at the bank and paying fees, an=
d the bank would stop making money, and it would no longer be able to pay f=
or real estate or tellers or vaults or executive bonuses. It would go out o=
f business in fairly short order. But it would not go out of business=C2=A0=
that minute. It would actually have enough money to give all the customers =
their money back, because it kept all the customers=E2=80=99 money in their=
 own envelopes the whole time.
No actual bank works that way.=C2=A0Real banks take deposits but don=E2=80=
=99t keep the money in envelopes; they lend it out.
  [4]
=C2=A0Most classically, they borrow short to lend long, taking checking dep=
osits that can be withdrawn at any time, and using them to make long-term m=
ortgages. This makes them vulnerable to runs,  Diamond-Dybvig, It=E2=80=99s=
 a Wonderful Life, etc., everyone knows all this.
But in theory=C2=A0a cryptocurrency exchange could work that way, and at a =
high level of generality Coinbase sort of does.
  [5]
=C2=A0Historically =E2=80=94=C2=A0not so much now, but until early this yea=
r anyway =E2=80=94=C2=A0cryptocurrencies were volatile and exciting and peo=
ple were jazzed to trade them a lot, so you could make a lot of money by ju=
st charging fees without doing anything else with customer assets. And that=
 is a run-proof business. If everyone takes their money out at once,=C2=A0y=
ou have the money.
But then one day a customer comes to you and says =E2=80=9CI have $10,000, =
but I am really bullish on Bitcoin, so I would like to buy $20,000 worth of=
 Bitcoin. Why don=E2=80=99t you lend me $10,000 so I can buy=C2=A0$20,000 o=
f Bitcoin, so I can get more excitement?=E2=80=9D This is called a margin l=
oan.
Or =E2=80=94=C2=A0equivalently =E2=80=94=C2=A0a customer comes to you and s=
ays =E2=80=9CI have $20,000 of Bitcoin in my account, and I need some cash =
this month. I don=E2=80=99t want to sell my Bitcoin, because I am a true be=
liever and also do not want to realize gains for tax purposes. Could you ju=
st=C2=A0lend=C2=A0me $10,000, secured by my $20,000 of Bitcoin? You know I=
=E2=80=99m good for it: If I don=E2=80=99t pay you back, you can sell my Bi=

  [7] As a legal/contractual matter, you may or may not have =E2=80=9Crecou=
rse=E2=80=9D against her =E2=80=94 you may or may not be able to sue her fo=
r the extra money=E2=80=94 but as a practical matter you are a crypto excha=
nge, don=E2=80=99t count on getting that money back.


  [8] In modern US stock markets, buybacks are the principal way of returni=
ng profits to shareholders, meaning that the connection between stock price=
s and corporate profits is in practice =E2=80=9Cwhen there are profits the =
company buys back stock=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94 just like with FTT.


  [9] They=E2=80=99ll probably lend you like $100 against $200 of their sto=
ck, in an ordinary-course transaction; they just won=E2=80=99t do too much =
of it.

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">FTX</h2>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">So how could <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloo=
mbergbusiness.com/click/29648502.256510/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9=
vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMTEtMDgvYmluYW5jZS1zLXpoYW8tc2JmLWVkLWZ0eC1zLW=
JhbmttYW4tZnJpZWQ_Y21waWQ9QkJEMTEwOTIyX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpb=
CZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjIxMTA5JnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25l=
eXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB2d902925" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscop=
e=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration:=
 none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; b=
order-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  this</a> happen? I do=
n=E2=80=99t know, but let me speculate a little bit.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Let=E2=80=99s start with Coinbase. Coinbase Gl=
obal Inc. runs a cryptocurrency exchange. When FTX.com, one of the largest =
crypto exchanges, was instantaneously vaporized yesterday, <a href=3D"https=
://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/29648502.256510/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuY2=
9pbmJhc2UuY29tL2Jsb2cvb3VyLWFwcHJvYWNoLXRvLXRyYW5zcGFyZW5jeS1yaXNrLW1hbmFnZ=
W1lbnQtYW5kLWNvbnN1bWVyLXByb3RlY3Rpb24/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB475d95f8" i=
temprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"co=
lor: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; b=
order-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: =
#ccc;"> Coinbase put out a statement</a>, the gist of which was =E2=80=9Cdo=
n=E2=80=99t worry,=C2=A0<em>we</em> are not going to be instantaneously vap=
orized.=E2=80=9D The part that I want to focus on is this paragraph:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">There can=E2=80=99t be a =E2=80=9C=
run on the bank=E2=80=9D at Coinbase. As you can review in our publicly fil=
ed, audited financial statements, we hold customer assets 1:1. Any institut=
ional lending activity at Coinbase is at the discretion of the customer and=
 backed by collateral. We have no gating for client loan recalls or withdra=
wals.</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The way it works is roughly that you open an a=
ccount and send dollars to Coinbase, and then you tell Coinbase =E2=80=9CI=
=E2=80=99d like to buy some Bitcoin with those dollars,=E2=80=9D and Coinba=
se buys Bitcoin and holds on to it for you=C2=A0and <a href=3D"https://link=
.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/29648502.256510/aHR0cHM6Ly9oZWxwLmNvaW5iY=
XNlLmNvbS9lbi9jb2luYmFzZS90cmFkaW5nLWFuZC1mdW5kaW5nL3ByaWNpbmctYW5kLWZlZXMv=
ZmVlcw/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB4ec08b3c" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=
=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: =
none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bo=
rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> charges you a fee</a> f=
or that transaction. You can check your account balance, and Coinbase says =
=E2=80=9Cyou have 0.5 Bitcoin=E2=80=9D or whatever. That 0.5=C2=A0Bitcoin i=
s, in the general case,=C2=A0<em>held by Coinbase</em>; it has possession o=
f the Bitcoin.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-1" style=3D"col=
or: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; bo=
rder-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; backgroun=
d-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[1]</span>
</a>=C2=A0But it is held in a custody account=C2=A0<em>for you</em>. <a hre=
f=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/29648502.256510/aHR0cHM6=
Ly93d3cuY29pbmJhc2UuY29tL2Jsb2cvc2V0dGluZy10aGUtcmVjb3JkLXN0cmFpZ2h0LXlvdXI=
tZnVuZHMtYXJlLXNhZmUtYXQtY29pbmJhc2UtYW5kLWFsd2F5cy13aWxsLWJl/607f07ceef4b8=
524a319a23cBf5b5e79e" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=
=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bord=
er-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: so=
lid; background-color: #ccc;"> Coinbase says</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Your funds are your funds, and your crypto is =
your crypto: Coinbase maintains internal systems, like a bank or a broker. =
Our fully audited ledger identifies your account, your fiat and crypto hold=
ings, and tracks your account activity in real time. There=E2=80=99s never =
a situation where customer funds could be confused with corporate assets.</=
p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">We will never repurpose your funds: We do not =
lend or take any action with your assets, unless you specifically instruct =
us to. Many banks and financial institutions use customer funds for commerc=
ial purposes including lending and trading, meaning that they often hold on=
ly a fraction of their customer assets at any given time. Coinbase always h=
olds customer assets 1:1. This means that funds are available to our custom=
ers 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, 365 days of the year.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The analogy is: Imagine a weird sort of bank. =
You come to the bank with $100 in paper bills, and you deposit it in the ba=
nk, and the bank takes your paper bills and sticks them in an envelope with=
 your name on it. Then it sticks the envelope in a vault, and if at any poi=
nt you ask for your money back, it opens the vault and hands you your envel=
ope. This sounds like a bad business model: The bank needs to pay for real =
estate and tellers and vaults, and it is not doing anything with your money=
. But the other weird thing about this bank is that, every day, you come in=
 and say =E2=80=9Chey I=E2=80=99d like to exchange my dollars for euros=E2=
=80=9D or =E2=80=9Cmy euros for pounds=E2=80=9D or whatever, and each time =
you do that the bank charges you a dollar. So you have $100, which you exch=
ange for =E2=82=AC99, which you exchange for =C2=A398, which you exchange f=
or $97, etc.,<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-2" style=3D"colo=
r: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; bor=
der-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background=
-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[2]</span>
</a>=C2=A0paying the bank $1 each time. If all of the bank=E2=80=99s custom=
ers do this every day, then the bank makes plenty of money to pay for real =
estate and tellers and vaults and executive bonuses, without doing anything=
 else with your money. It just takes the $100 out of your envelope and repl=
aces it with =E2=82=AC99, etc., always keeping exactly the right amount of =
money (in whatever currency you like that day) in exactly your envelope.<a =
class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-3" style=3D"color: #000000; text=
-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color=
: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transpar=
ent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[3]</span>
</a></p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And then if one day every single customer walk=
ed into the bank at the same time and said =E2=80=9Cwe would like our money=
 back,=E2=80=9D the bank would just hand them all their envelopes. Don=E2=
=80=99t get me wrong, this would be a catastrophe for the bank: If everyone=
 took their envelopes back, then presumably they would stop changing money =
at the bank and paying fees, and the bank would stop making money, and it w=
ould no longer be able to pay for real estate or tellers or vaults or execu=
tive bonuses. It would go out of business in fairly short order. But it wou=
ld not go out of business=C2=A0<em>that minute</em>. It would actually have=
 enough money to give all the customers their money back, because it kept a=
ll the customers=E2=80=99 money in their own envelopes the whole time.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">No actual <em>bank</em> works that way.=C2=A0R=
eal banks take deposits but don=E2=80=99t keep the money in envelopes; they=
 lend it out.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-4" style=3D"colo=
r: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; bor=
der-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background=
-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[4]</span>
</a>=C2=A0Most classically, they borrow short to lend long, taking checking=
 deposits that can be withdrawn at any time, and using them to make long-te=
rm mortgages. This makes them vulnerable to runs, <a href=3D"https://link.m=
ail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/29648502.256510/aHR0cHM6Ly9lbi53aWtpcGVkaWE=
ub3JnL3dpa2kvRGlhbW9uZCVFMiU4MCU5M0R5YnZpZ19tb2RlbA/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23=
cB98448892" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank=
" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-=
width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backg=
round-color: #ccc;"> Diamond-Dybvig</a>, <em>It=E2=80=99s a Wonderful Life<=
/em>, etc., everyone knows all this.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But in theory=C2=A0a <em>cryptocurrency exchan=
ge</em> could work that way, and at a high level of generality Coinbase sor=
t of does.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-5" style=3D"color: =
#000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border=
-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-co=
lor: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[5]</span>
</a>=C2=A0Historically =E2=80=94=C2=A0not so much now, but until early this=
 year anyway =E2=80=94=C2=A0cryptocurrencies were volatile and exciting and=
 people were jazzed to trade them a lot, so you could make a lot of money b=
y just charging fees without doing anything else with customer assets. And =
that is a run-proof business. If everyone takes their money out at once,=C2=
=A0<em>you have the money</em>.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But then one day a customer comes to you and s=
ays =E2=80=9CI have $10,000, but I am really bullish on Bitcoin, so I would=
 like to buy $20,000 worth of Bitcoin. Why don=E2=80=99t you lend me $10,00=
0 so I can buy=C2=A0$20,000 of Bitcoin, so I can get more excitement?=E2=80=
=9D This is called a margin loan.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Or =E2=80=94=C2=A0equivalently =E2=80=94=C2=A0=
a customer comes to you and says =E2=80=9CI have $20,000 of Bitcoin in my a=
ccount, and I need some cash this month. I don=E2=80=99t want to sell my Bi=
tcoin, because I am a true believer and also do not want to realize gains f=
or tax purposes. Could you just=C2=A0<em>lend=C2=A0</em>me $10,000, secured=
 by my $20,000 of Bitcoin? You know I=E2=80=99m good for it: If I don=E2=80=
=99t pay you back, you can sell my Bitcoin and pay yourself back from the p=
roceeds.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">You might just say =E2=80=9Cno, that=E2=80=99s=
 dumb, Bitcoin is volatile, buying $10,000 of Bitcoin is plenty of exciteme=
nt.=E2=80=9D (In fact Coinbase <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusine=
ss.com/click/29648502.256510/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuY29pbmRlc2suY29tL21hcmtldHMvMj=

"> shut down margin trading</a> in 2020.) But your competitors probably off=
er loans, and it is tempting for you to do it too. So you say, sure, fine, =
I=E2=80=99ll take your $10,000 and put $20,000 of Bitcoin in your account.<=
/p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But where do you get the money that you are le=
nding to the customer? Well, you have to borrow it too. Ordinarily the way =
that you will borrow it is by putting up the customer=E2=80=99s Bitcoin as =
collateral to your lender, just as the customer puts up its Bitcoin as coll=
ateral to you. If the customer defaults, you still have to pay your lender =
(and then you get the Bitcoin back and can sell it to pay off your customer=
=E2=80=99s liability to you); if you default, the lender sells the Bitcoin.=
</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But who are the lenders? Oh, various possibili=
ties. But one general point is that while some customers will want to borro=
w dollars to buy Bitcoin, other customers will want to=C2=A0<em>borrow Bitc=
oin.=C2=A0</em>One reason to borrow Bitcoin is to=C2=A0<em>buy dollars</em>=
, that is, to=C2=A0<em>short Bitcoin:=C2=A0</em>I borrow one Bitcoin, I sel=
l it for $20,000, a week later Bitcoin drops to $18,000, I buy back the one=
 Bitcoin for $18,000, I return it to my lender and I keep the $2,000. There=
 are variations on this trade (I borrow Bitcoin and sell it for Ethereum, b=
etting on the relative value between the tokens, etc.). It is necessarily a=
 leveraged trade; I can=E2=80=99t short Bitcoin without borrowing it.<a cla=
ss=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-6" style=3D"color: #000000; text-de=
coration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #=
000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent=
 !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[6]</span>
</a></p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you are a crypto=C2=A0<em>exchange</em>, th=
is is a nice opportunity. You have Customer A who has Bitcoin and wants to =
borrow dollars, and Customer B who has dollars and wants to borrow Bitcoin.=
 (By =E2=80=9Cdollars,=E2=80=9D for a crypto exchange, I mostly mean =E2=80=
=9Cdollar-denominated stablecoins,=E2=80=9D though potentially also dollars=
.) You take some of Customer A=E2=80=99s Bitcoin and lend it to Customer B,=
 and you take some of Customer B=E2=80=99s dollars and lend them to Custome=
r A. Each of them is overcollateralized =E2=80=94=C2=A0you only lend Custom=
er A half the value of her Bitcoin, and you only lend Customer B half the v=
alue of his dollars =E2=80=94=C2=A0so you feel pretty safe. And they both p=
ay you interest.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But there are risks. One day Customer A might =
come in, pay off her dollar loan, and ask to take her Bitcoin back. You don=
=E2=80=99t have her Bitcoin, or not all of them anyway; some of them are wi=
th Customer B. Customer B=C2=A0<em>owes=C2=A0</em>them to you =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0ultimately you=E2=80=99re good for it =E2=80=94=C2=A0but you don=E2=80=
=99t have them now. There is a timing problem.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The solution to this is pretty much to have so=
me extra cash =E2=80=94=C2=A0some of your own capital =E2=80=94=C2=A0to bri=
dge these timing problems. Eventually you=E2=80=99ll get the rest of the Bi=
tcoin=C2=A0back from Customer B, but for now you just pay Customer A out of=
 your own Bitcoin stash.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But the timing problem is also connected to a =
real economic risk. If the price of Bitcoin falls by 90%, Customer B will b=
e thrilled. He will come to you and say =E2=80=9Chere=E2=80=99s my=C2=A0Bit=
coin back, I=E2=80=99d like to withdraw my dollars.=E2=80=9D But you don=E2=
=80=99t have his dollars, or not all of them; half of them are with Custome=
r A. Your dollar loan to Customer A is now underwater: You loaned her 50% o=
f the value of her Bitcoin, but Bitcoin fell by 90%, so she owes you more t=
han her collateral is worth. You call her up and ask her for more money =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0a =E2=80=9Cmargin call=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94 but she, sensibly,=C2=
=A0doesn=E2=80=99t answer the phone.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#fo=
otnote-7" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border=
-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none=
 !important; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-bloc=
k;">
<span>[7]</span>
</a>=C2=A0You have to pay Customer B out of your own capital, and you don=
=E2=80=99t get it back from Customer A. You've just lost money. Actually th=
at=E2=80=99s the best outcome. The worst outcome is that you don=E2=80=99t =
have enough capital, you go bankrupt, and Customer B does not get his money=
 back.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Everyone knows=C2=A0<em>this</em>, which is wh=
y crypto exchanges =E2=80=94=C2=A0and securities broker-dealers, who have t=
he same basic business model =E2=80=94=C2=A0spend most of their time thinki=
ng about risk management. Before the price of Bitcoin drops too far, you wi=
ll be calling up Customer A for more margin, and if she doesn=E2=80=99t ans=
wer the phone you will liquidate her position to pay back the loan you made=
. If you are a sophisticated modern crypto exchange like FTX, you will have=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But sometimes market moves are catastrophic, a=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Two big lessons:=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">1: Never use a token you created as collateral=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">2: Don=E2=80=99t borrow if you run a crypto bu=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CNever use a token you created as coll=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One other point here is that if this is the st=
ory, then it is not a=C2=A0<em>liquidity=C2=A0</em>crisis but a=C2=A0<em>so=
lvency=C2=A0</em>one. That is, the problem is not a timing mismatch, in whi=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Changpeng Zhao moved fast when Sam Bankman-Fri=

y further crypto contagion.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Within hours, he was forced to reconsider.=C2=
=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">For starters, Binance executives quickly found=
 themselves staring into a financial black hole -- a gap between liabilitie=
s and assets at FTX that=E2=80=99s probably in the billions, and possibly m=
ore than $6 billion, according to a person familiar with the matter.=C2=A0<=
/p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On top of that, US regulators are circling FTX=
, investigating whether the firm properly handled customer funds, as well a=
s its relationship with other parts of Bankman-Fried=E2=80=99s crypto empir=
e, Bloomberg News reported Wednesday.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It makes for a tricky decision for Zhao, known=
 in the crypto world as CZ: Follow through with rescuing his onetime top ri=
val and shoulder the financial and regulatory burdens, or let FTX crumble a=
nd sort through the potential wreckage? Zhao himself admits there was no =
=E2=80=9Cmaster plan=E2=80=9D to take over FTX.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">His answer, at least for now, is that the fina=
ncial hole appears too deep. Binance is unlikely to follow through on its t=
akeover of FTX, according to the person familiar, who wasn=E2=80=99t author=
ized to publicly discuss the matter.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Seems bad.</p>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Do you think that there is a big financial fir=
m in the US that got through the entire pandemic without any employees doin=
g any business over WhatsApp or text messages from their personal phone? It=
 seems somewhat unlikely. So much business is about personal relationships,=
 and texting and WhatsApp can feel more personal than your firm email accou=
nt. And since you were not visiting clients in person during the pandemic, =
or sitting in your office, you might have been a bit more inclined to use y=
our personal cell phone to communicate.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The US Securities and Exchange Commission and =
Commodity Futures Trading Commission have=C2=A0<a href=3D"https://link.mail=

portant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bot=
tom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">concluded that that=E2=80=99s il=
legal</a>, and fined all the banks for doing it, because they all did it. <=
a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/29648502.256510/aHR=
0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDktMjgvdGhlLW=

 fine is $200 million per bank</a> (SEC and CFTC combined). I mean, there=
=E2=80=99s a little bit of differentiation, but not too much. Almost all=C2=

of fine for smaller banks. Nobody cares very much about how culpable each b=
ank is, or how many bad messages it sent. (With one exception: Bank of Amer=
ica Corp. paid a bit more for being a bit more culpable.) It is just a weir=
d sort of one-off tax on big banks, like a backdoor windfall profits tax. =
=E2=80=9CWhatsApp, everyone pay $200 million.=E2=80=9D Okay.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Anyway <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergb=

<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Tesla Inc. Chief Executive Officer Elon Musk s=
old at least $3.95 billion of the electric-vehicle maker=E2=80=99s shares j=
ust days after closing his buyout of Twitter Inc.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Musk unloaded 19.5 million shares, according t=
o regulatory filings on Tuesday in New York, his first disposals since Augu=
st. The documents didn=E2=80=99t indicate that the transactions were pre-pl=
anned.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The world=E2=80=99s richest person followed th=
rough with his takeover of the social-media platform in October, after spen=
ding months trying to get out of it. In August, Musk had said he was done o=
ffloading Tesla stock and that it was important to avoid an =E2=80=9Cemerge=
ncy sale=E2=80=9D of the shares in case he was forced to close the Twitter =
acquisition and struggled to bring in additional equity partners.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">What is he doing with the money? Some possibil=
ities:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">He used 100% of the proceeds of his p=
revious sales to pay for Twitter, and now is selling more to pay his year-e=
nd tax bill=C2=A0on those previous sales.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom=
: 5px;">He got to closing and realized he didn=E2=80=99t actually have enou=
gh cash to pay for Twitter, so <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusine=
ss.com/click/29648502.256510/aHR0cHM6Ly90d2l0dGVyLmNvbS9JdmFuVGhlSy9zdGF0dX=
MvMTU5MDE1MzY1NDA4MjA4ODk2MQ/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB1daabded" itemprop=3D=
"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #0000=
00; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bott=
om-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> so=
meone loaned him the money</a> for a week and now he is paying it back.</li=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Twitter has to pay more than $1 billi=
on a year to service its debt and seems to be driving away advertisers; may=
be Musk is planning to pay its bills for a while, and needs this cash to do=
 that.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Having closed the deal for =
Twitter and spent a few days wreaking havoc, Musk has gotten bored and move=
d on to some other expensive hobby, which we will hear about in due time.</=
li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Index funds?</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One popular, odd theory of Musk=E2=80=99s deal=
 for Twitter is that he just wanted to diversify his Tesla holdings. Simply=
 selling billions of dollars of Tesla stock would have upset his fans, driv=
en down the stock price and undermined his image as a committed true believ=
er. But selling billions of dollars of Tesla stock=C2=A0<em>to fund a weird=
 quest to buy Twitter=C2=A0</em>would =E2=80=A6=C2=A0I mean, be a distracti=
on at least.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I think economically that theory makes very li=
ttle sense, given that Musk has lost billions of dollars on every part of t=
his trade: He ended up =E2=80=9Cobviously overpaying for Twitter,=E2=80=9D =
<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/29648502.256510/aH=
R0cHM6Ly93d3cud3NqLmNvbS9hcnRpY2xlcy9lbG9uLW11c2stc2VsbHMtYWxtb3N0LTQtYmlsb=
Glvbi1vZi10ZXNsYS1zdG9jay1hZnRlci1jb21wbGV0aW5nLXR3aXR0ZXItdGFrZW92ZXItMTE2=
Njc5NTc5ODM_bW9kPWhwX2xlYWRfcG9zMQ/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB598a55c4" itemp=
rop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color:=
 #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; borde=
r-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc=
;"> in his words</a>, and Tesla=E2=80=99s stock is down 50% since he starte=
d messing with Twitter. But it sure has been a distraction! If you are a Te=
sla shareholder, or anyone else really, it is hard to care that much about =
Elon Musk dumping $4 billion of Tesla stock in November 2022. He=C2=A0just =
has so much else going on.</p>
